title: Special Matrix Structures
objectives: Recognize symmetric positive definite matrices and their properties
Implement Cholesky decomposition for SPD systems
Exploit tridiagonal structure using the Thomas algorithm
Understand banded and sparse matrix representations
Apply efficient solvers for structured systems

content.text:
Many applications produce matrices with special structure that allows 
more efficient and stable algorithms.

content.title: Symmetric Positive Definite Matrix
content.content:
A matrix $A \in \R^{n \times n}$ is **symmetric positive definite** 
(SPD) if:

1. $A = A^T$ (symmetric)

2. $\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ (positive definite)

content.title: Properties of SPD Matrices
content.content:
If $A$ is symmetric positive definite, then:

1. All eigenvalues of $A$ are positive

2. All diagonal entries are positive: $a_{ii} > 0$

3. All leading principal minors are positive

4. $A$ has a unique Cholesky decomposition

5. Gaussian elimination without pivoting is stable

proof
(1) If $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$ with $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$, then 
$\lambda\|\mathbf{v}\|^2 = \mathbf{v}^T A\mathbf{v} > 0$, so $\lambda > 0$.

(2) Take $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{e}_i$ (the $i$-th standard basis vector): 
$a_{ii} = \mathbf{e}_i^T A \mathbf{e}_i > 0$.

(3) Any principal submatrix of an SPD matrix is also SPD.

(4)–(5) See Cholesky decomposition below.
proof
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Testing Positive Definiteness
content.problem:
Is $A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$ positive definite?

**Method 1: Eigenvalues**

The characteristic polynomial is $\det(A - \lambda I) = -\lambda^3 + 15\lambda^2 - 62\lambda + 68$.
The eigenvalues are approximately $\lambda_1 \approx 8.5$, $\lambda_2 \approx 4.5$, 
$\lambda_3 \approx 2.0$. All positive, so $A$ is SPD.

**Method 2: Leading Principal Minors**
$$
\det(A_{1:1,1:1}) &= 4 > 0 \\
\det(A_{1:2,1:2}) &= 4 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 2 = 16 > 0 \\
\det(A_{1:3,1:3}) &= 4(30-4) - 2(12-2) + 1(4-5) = 104 - 20 - 1 = 83 > 0
$$
All positive, confirming $A$ is SPD.

content.title: Cholesky Decomposition
content.content:
The **Cholesky decomposition** of a symmetric positive definite matrix 
$A$ is the factorization:
\[
A = LL^T
\]
where $L$ is lower triangular with positive diagonal entries.

content.title: Existence and Uniqueness of Cholesky
content.content:
Every symmetric positive definite matrix has a unique Cholesky decomposition.

proof
**Existence:** We construct $L$ column by column. For column $j$:
\[
l_{jj} = \sqrt{a_{jj} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} l_{jk}^2}, \quad
l_{ij} = \frac{1}{l_{jj}}\left(a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} l_{ik}l_{jk}\right) \text{ for } i > j
\]

The expression under the square root is positive because the $(j,j)$ entry 
of the reduced matrix (Schur complement) is positive for SPD matrices.

**Uniqueness:** If $A = L_1L_1^T = L_2L_2^T$, then 
$L_2^{-1}L_1 = L_2^T L_1^{-T}$. The left side is lower triangular; the 
right side is upper triangular. Hence both are diagonal. Since $L_1$ and 
$L_2$ have positive diagonal entries, we must have $L_2^{-1}L_1 = I$.
proof
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Cholesky Decomposition
content.content:
algorithmic[1]
 Symmetric positive definite matrix $A \in \R^{n \times n}$
 Lower triangular $L$ with $A = LL^T$
$j = 1$ **to** $n$
 $s \gets 0$
 $k = 1$ **to** $j-1$
 $s \gets s + l_{jk}^2$
 
 $l_{jj} \gets \sqrt{a_{jj} - s}$
 $i = j+1$ **to** $n$
 $s \gets 0$
 $k = 1$ **to** $j-1$
 $s \gets s + l_{ik} \cdot l_{jk}$
 
 $l_{ij} \gets (a_{ij} - s) / l_{jj}$
 

 $L$
algorithmic

content.title: Computing Cholesky Decomposition
content.problem:
Find the Cholesky decomposition of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Solution.**

**Column 1:**
\[
l_{11} = \sqrt{a_{11}} = \sqrt{4} = 2
\]
\[
l_{21} = \frac{a_{21}}{l_{11}} = \frac{2}{2} = 1
\]

**Column 2:**
\[
l_{22} = \sqrt{a_{22} - l_{21}^2} = \sqrt{5 - 1} = 2
\]

**Result:**
\[
L = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
\]

**Verify:**
\[
LL^T = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix} = A \checkmark
\]

content.title: Tridiagonal Matrix
content.content:
A **tridiagonal matrix** has nonzero entries only on the main diagonal 
and the two adjacent diagonals:
\[
A = \begin{pmatrix}
b_1 & c_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
a_2 & b_2 & c_2 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & a_3 & b_3 & \ddots & \vdots \\
\vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots & c_{n-1} \\
0 & \cdots & 0 & a_n & b_n
\end{pmatrix}
\]

items: Finite difference methods for differential equations

content.title: Thomas Algorithm Complexity
content.content:
A tridiagonal system can be solved in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ operations, compared 
to $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ for general matrices.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Thomas Algorithm (Tridiagonal Solver)
content.content:
algorithmic[1]
 Tridiagonal coefficients $a_i, b_i, c_i$ and right-hand side $d_i$
 Solution vector $x_i$
 Forward sweep: LU decomposition
 $c'_1 \gets c_1 / b_1$
 $d'_1 \gets d_1 / b_1$
$i = 2$ **to** $n$
 $w \gets b_i - a_i \cdot c'_{i-1}$
 $c'_i \gets c_i / w$ Only if $i < n$
 $d'_i \gets (d_i - a_i \cdot d'_{i-1}) / w$

 Back substitution
 $x_n \gets d'_n$
$i = n-1$ **down to** $1$
 $x_i \gets d'_i - c'_i \cdot x_{i+1}$

 $\mathbf{x}$
algorithmic

content.title: Banded Matrix
content.content:
A matrix $A$ has **bandwidth** $2p + 1$ if $a_{ij} = 0$ whenever 
$|i - j| > p$. The **lower bandwidth** is $p$ and the **upper bandwidth** 
is $q$ if $a_{ij} = 0$ for $i - j > p$ and $j - i > q$.

content.title: LU of Banded Matrices
content.content:
If $A$ has lower bandwidth $p$ and upper bandwidth $q$, then (without pivoting):

- $L$ has lower bandwidth $p$

- $U$ has upper bandwidth $q$

- Operation count: $\mathcal{O}(npq)$ instead of $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Pentadiagonal Matrix
content.problem:
A **pentadiagonal** matrix (bandwidth 5) has the form:
\[
A = \begin{pmatrix}
b_1 & c_1 & e_1 & 0 & \cdots \\
a_2 & b_2 & c_2 & e_2 & \cdots \\
d_3 & a_3 & b_3 & c_3 & \ddots \\
0 & d_4 & a_4 & \ddots & \ddots \\
\vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots
\end{pmatrix}
\]
Such systems arise from fourth-order finite differences and can be solved 
in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ operations.

content.title: Sparse Matrix
content.content:
A matrix is **sparse** if most of its entries are zero. Typically, 
a matrix with $\mathcal{O}(n)$ nonzeros out of $n^2$ entries is considered sparse.

content.title: Sparse Matrix Storage
content.content:
Common storage formats for sparse matrices:

1. **Coordinate (COO):** Store $(i, j, a_{ij})$ triples for nonzeros

2. **Compressed Sparse Row (CSR):** Store row pointers, column indices, values

3. **Compressed Sparse Column (CSC):** Store column pointers, row indices, values

content.title: Fill-in During LU
content.content:
When computing the LU decomposition of a sparse matrix, new nonzeros may 
appear in $L$ and $U$ that were zeros in $A$. This **fill-in** can 
dramatically increase storage and operation count. Reordering rows and 
columns (e.g., using minimum degree or nested dissection algorithms) can 
minimize fill-in.

content.title: Fill-in Example
content.problem:
Consider the "arrow" matrix:
\[
A = \begin{pmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & 0 & 3 & 0 \\
1 & 0 & 0 & 4
\end{pmatrix}
\]

$A$ has 10 nonzeros. After LU decomposition:
\[
L = \begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & -1 & 1 & 0 \\
1 & -1 & -1 & 1
\end{pmatrix}, \quad
U = \begin{pmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
0 & 1 & -1 & -1 \\
0 & 0 & 3 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 3
\end{pmatrix}
\]

$L$ and $U$ together have 16 nonzeros—60\% fill-in!

content.title: Block Matrix
Block LU Decomposition
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
Block methods are particularly effective when the blocks have special 
structure (e.g., dense blocks in an otherwise sparse matrix).
